content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN.

Since Chef's favorite number is 333, he wants to make all numbers in the array a multiple of 333.

Chef can do the following operation:


Select any 222 different indices iii and jjj and increase AiA_iAi​ by 111 and decrease AjA_jAj​ by 111.
Help Chef find out the minimum number of operations required (or report its not possible) to achieve his objective.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains NNN, denoting the length of the array.
The second line of each testcase contains NNN space separated integers AiA_iAi​.


Output Format
Output the minimum number of operations required to make all numbers divisible by 333.


If it is not possible to make every number divisible by 333, then output −1-1−1.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case starts with an integer N, the length of the array. The next line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to make every array element a multiple of 3. If it is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: residues are [1,2,0]; one pair (1,2) resolves both in one operation.
Test case 2: all numbers already divisible by 3, no operation needed.
Test case 3: total sum modulo 3 is 1, impossible to reach all multiples of 3.
c1 = 3, c2 = 3. Pair each 1 with a 2 → 3 operations. No leftovers.
c1 = 0, c2 = 3. No pairs. Three elements with remainder 2 need 2 operations (group of three).

solution.approach:
For each element compute its remainder modulo 3. Let c1 be the count of elements with remainder 1 and c2 the count with remainder 2. The total sum modulo 3 equals (c1 + 2·c2) mod 3. If this value is not 0, the target configuration (all remainders 0) is unreachable, so answer is -1.
If reachable, we can pair a remainder‑1 element with a remainder‑2 element in one operation (decrease the 1‑element, increase the 2‑element). This resolves both residues, costing min(c1, c2) operations.
After all possible pairs are used, only one type of residue may remain. Because the overall sum is 0 modulo 3, the remaining count is a multiple of 3. Three equal residues can be fixed in two operations (e.g., 1→0, 1→2, then 2→0). Hence each group of three leftover elements needs 2 operations. The number of leftover elements is |c1‑c2|, so extra operations = 2·(|c1‑c2|/3).
Total minimum operations = min(c1, c2) + 2·(|c1‑c2|/3). The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the array length. Overall O(total N) ≤ O(2·10^5).
